one_line_takeaway: An ensemble of many small (86M-parameter) fine-tuned Prompt Guard classifiers, combined via a Random-Forest-predicted expert plus stochastic peer sampling, matches or beats billion-parameter black-box/white-box moderation baselines (F1 0.922 vs. 0.208-0.847) while being incrementally updatable via single-model fine-tuning rather than full retraining.
key_finding: At k=9, n=5 (430M effective parameters, a 45% reduction from using the full k=9 ensemble), BAGEL achieves ASR (attack success rate, defined as false-negative rate) = 0.095, FPR = 0.066, F1 = 0.922, outperforming all compared baselines' F1 scores: ToxicDetector (ASR 0.045, FPR 0.326, F1 0.847, 300M+7B params), ShieldGemma-2B (ASR 0.624, FPR 0.038, F1 0.534), OpenAI Moderation API (ASR 0.881, FPR 0.024, F1 0.208, param count unknown), Perspective API (ASR 0.569, FPR 0.068, F1 0.642, param count unknown), and LastLayer (ASR 0.598, FPR 0.171, F1 0.519, non-deep-learning). Performance saturates around n=5 (going from n=5 to n=9 only reduces ASR from 0.096 to 0.080 and FPR from 0.067 to 0.051). The router+stochastic-selection strategy tracks closely with the oracle 'Ideal' strategy despite the Random Forest router's own classification accuracy being only ~80.6% (at k=9), indicating the random peer selections compensate for router misclassifications. Across incremental updates (k=3 to k=9), final F1 never drops below 0.92, though when the largest/final dataset is added, single-expert (n=1) predictions become notably unbalanced, and aggregation is needed to restabilize performance. Pruning the 9 router features to 5 (via 0.7 dendrogram distance threshold on Spearman/Ward clustering) only reduces router accuracy from 0.806 to 0.794.
core_contribution: The paper introduces BAGEL (Bootstrap AGgregated Ensemble Layer), a malicious-LLM-prompt detection framework built as an ensemble of small (86M-parameter) fine-tuned Prompt Guard 2 classifiers ('promptcops'), each specialized on a distinct attack dataset. At inference, an interpretable Random Forest router (trained on 9 lightweight structural features of the prompt, e.g. prompt_length, whitespace_proportion, special_char_proportion, shannon_entropy) predicts the single most-suitable promptcop, and n-1 additional promptcops are sampled uniformly at random from the remaining ensemble; their probability outputs are simply averaged and thresholded (tau, calibrated via a two-stage coarse-to-fine search) to produce a benign/malicious decision. New attack types are incorporated by fine-tuning one new 86M-parameter promptcop on the new dataset and adding it to the ensemble, plus re-calibrating the router and threshold on a cumulative global calibration set -- avoiding full-system retraining. This modifies classical bootstrap aggregation (training members on disjoint attack-type datasets rather than resampled subsets of one dataset) and mixture-of-experts (routing to a router-picked expert plus stochastically sampled peers rather than to the argmax expert alone).
threat_model: Attacker submits a malicious prompt directly into an LLM application's chat interface to elicit objectionable/policy-violating content or instructions for illegal activity. The attacker has no access to the underlying system prompt or implementation details (remote-served LLM) and may range from a novice issuing direct harmful requests to an experienced adversary using jailbreak techniques (role-play, intent obfuscation, instruction-hierarchy override) or prompt injection (malicious directive concealed within an ostensibly benign task). Attackers are assumed able to draw on publicly available/community-shared jailbreak techniques and novel variations of established attack patterns. The defender's goal is binary classification (benign vs. malicious) applied before the prompt reaches the LLM; the defender has prior exposure to the general categories of attacks (simple harmful, jailbreak, prompt injection) but not necessarily to specific novel instances. The paper explicitly scopes out multi-turn conversational attacks and fine-grained policy-violation sub-classification.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) binary classification only -- no fine-grained attack/policy-category sub-classification, unlike OpenAI Moderation or Perspective; (2) evaluation is restricted to single-turn/isolated prompts -- multi-turn conversational attacks where malicious intent is distributed across turns are untested; (3) BAGEL's adaptability depends on the base model (Prompt Guard 2) having been pre-trained on broadly relevant attack classes (jailbreak/injection), so it is an open question whether entirely novel attack paradigms outside these established categories would transfer; (4) the Random Forest router relies on generic prompt-structure features that could in principle be evaded by an adversary aware of the router's feature set (mimicking benign structural statistics), though this adaptive/adversarial-evasion scenario against the router itself is not directly tested by the authors.

datasets_used: Total corpus: 839,140 samples; combined held-out test set at kmax=9: 167,828 samples
quantitative_results: BAGEL (k=9, n=5, 430M effective params): ASR 0.095, FPR 0.066, F1 0.922
n sweep at k=9: ASR falls from 0.096 (n=5) to 0.080 (n=9); FPR falls from 0.067 (n=5) to 0.051 (n=9); ~45% inference cost reduction at n=5 vs n=9
Random Forest router accuracy: 0.806 at k=9 (drops to 0.761 at k=7, 0.783 at k=8, rises to 0.976 at k=3, 0.957 at k=4, 0.953 at k=5, 0.935 at k=6)
Feature pruning: reducing router input from 9 to 5 features (via Spearman correlation + Ward hierarchical clustering, 0.7 distance threshold) reduces router accuracy from 0.806 to only 0.794
baselines_compared: OpenAI Moderation API: ASR 0.881, FPR 0.024, F1 0.208, params unknown, not fine-tunable
Perspective API: ASR 0.569, FPR 0.068, F1 0.642, params unknown, not fine-tunable
ToxicDetector: ASR 0.045, FPR 0.326, F1 0.847, 300M MLP + 7B LLaMA2 embedding backbone, fine-tunable
ShieldGemma (2B variant of Gemma 2): ASR 0.624, FPR 0.038, F1 0.534, fine-tunable
LastLayer: ASR 0.598, FPR 0.171, F1 0.519, structural/non-deep-learning security library, not fine-tunable

Internal baseline: single un-fine-tuned Prompt Guard 2 instance (used as the 'Baseline' curve in the selection-efficiency experiment, shown to perform much worse in ASR than fine-tuned promptcops since it is tailored mainly to jailbreaking prompts)
